story:
I love mashed potatoes—boring, white, fluffy, buttery, steaming from the pot. My eldest daughter, Jessica, loves them too. Because of a rare chromosome deletion and developmental disabilities, she can’t chew safely; the wrong texture can be a recipe for disaster. Mashed potatoes are her ideal food.

Recipe: Mashed Potatoes
Ingredients: 4 pounds russet potatoes; 2–3 garlic cloves (optional); 1/3 cup melted butter; about 1 cup milk, cream, water, or vegetable broth; salt and pepper. Peel and quarter potatoes; simmer in salted water or broth with the garlic until fork-tender. Drain well. Warm your liquid. Mash potatoes with butter, adding warm liquid until smooth and safe. Season. Serve hot.

Jessica was flagged as globally delayed at six months. At twenty-one, a newer genetic test finally pinpointed the issue: a deletion on chromosome 1 (1q43–q44). The label is really just a map of what’s missing, not a neat list of symptoms. In practice, Jess needs help with everything—morning to night. Yet she meets each day with a smile and a hum. Because she’s so easygoing, any sign of distress means we must listen.

At twenty-five, Jess moved into a group home two miles from us. She shares it with four women and has 24/7 staff. Their job includes preparing food to the correct texture. If there are hard, chewy, or chunky pieces, Jess can choke. Her meals are processed to a safe consistency. As her parents, we live by faith in those details—the ingredients of her care.

Thank you, Alex. One visit, years ago, before our current house manager, my son stopped by to see his sister. Jess was “off.” Her eyes were bulging and watery. She was congested, but it felt like more. The staff brushed it off as “sudden congestion.” Alex thought, How is congestion sudden? He called me at work and told me to go, now.

I reached the house fifteen minutes later and knew: Jess was in crisis. “Call 911!” I yelled. My husband pulled up as the aide dialed. Jess’s oxygen level looked fine on the pulse-ox, but her eyes were telling a different story.

EMTs came and whisked her to the ambulance. We followed. In triage, Jess suddenly coughed—hard—and out flew a fully intact red potato, like she’d expelled an organ. (Yes, we have photos. No, I will not share. You’d never eat potatoes again.) We still don’t know how her oxygen read normal or how she kept breathing with a whole spud lodged in her throat.

Phew.

The hospital monitored her. Soon she was cleared and back to her smiling, humming self. The rest of us? Mashed from the what-ifs. But our son was a hero. He trusted his instincts over a dismissive answer, and that made all the difference.

Afterward, new protocols were put in place. Every dish for Jess is checked for texture and piece-size before serving. That incident was in 2012; it hasn’t happened again. We’re grateful for the vigilant manager and staff she has now, and for family and friends who stay watchful. Even in a group home, Jess still needs intense family involvement.

Mashed potatoes remain my favorite food, and my reminder that things can change in a moment. Consistency and attentiveness are essential—when mashing spuds, and when caring for someone who cannot safely chew or speak up for herself.
